title: Lesson 301
transcript: We are at the network layer and as we said before, the network layer is really the captain of the team. There are some really important protocols that operate here that really drive and coordinate a lot of what happens in networking, specifically the IP protocol. The IP protocol is probably the captain of the TCP IP team and one of the things that we have to look at is the difference and one of the problems that the industry has identified and when they developed TCP IP decades ago They didn't foresee the growth that networking would grow and show to what it is today. And one of the problems that we have with IP version 4 is if you look at the header of a IP packet you'll see that there are some fields in here that really allow IP to do what it's supposed to do and it's two major functions are as you know fragmentation and addressing and so there is information in the header portion of the datagram or the packet that you see here listed so there are things like source ip address destination ip address some really relevant fields like time to live which gets decremented by one every time a router handles a datagram, and when it gets to zero, that packet is actually dropped. Imagine what would happen if we didn't have field like that Denali service attacks would last forever across the internet so that's a pretty useful field but so are the other ones that you see here now if you look at this IP version 4 is what we've been using ever since TCP IP was invented and developed and It is a 32-bit address that is used for source IP and destination IP address. And what they do is they divide those 32 bits into four groups of eight bits. That's why your IP addresses end up looking like four numbers separated by dots. There are no dots, we just put those in for readability purposes. But if you do the math, And you're going to see this on a slide coming up. 2 to the 32 only has a finite number of unique IP addresses that anything that is 32 bits. can represent, and that number is just under 4.3 billion. Now when you think about how big the internet is, everything that is the internet and connected to the internet has to have a unique IP address, which means something like that could never grow to more than 4.3 billion things connected to it. There are organizations out there that try and keep track of how many things are connected to the internet right now. And that number is much higher than 4.3 billion. In fact, they estimate that we are probably by now over 30 billion things that are connected to the Internet, which means that they each have to have unique logical addressing called an IP address. So how is that even possible? Well, it's because of something that we've already talked about, and that is NAT, Network Address Translation, allows organizations to allow a whole bunch of devices to go out with the same IP address. What keeps them unique is going to be their port numbers that are also specified. through NAT so that when the request comes back we can redirect it to the right machine. But that NAT really has saved us and from a security perspective it can also provide a way to hide what our internal addressing schemes actually look like. But this idea that we would run out of unique IP addresses really led to the development of what we call IP version 6. And IP version 6 has already been around for over a decade in fact a number of years and what they've done as you can see here we're trying to depict what the ip version 6 header and packet looks like you'll see that one of the things that they've done is they've expanded the addressing to 128 bits for both the source IP address and the destination IP address. So this is going to provide a whole bunch more unique IP addresses. There are already some organizations that are already using IP version six. In fact, it is. backwards compatible which means if companies are using it they can still talk to other companies and other networks that are still using IP version 4 because it is backwards compatible. And in fact, I believe as of Windows 7, which came out more than a decade ago, support for IPv6 was already built into that operating system, which makes it easy for companies but I think the industry has mandated that by some point in the future everybody has to use IP version 6 and so our IP addresses are going to look much different they're not going to be four numbers separated by dots they're going to look more like mac addresses although mac addresses are only 48 bits these addresses will be 128 bits they'll look more like hexadecimal numbers it's going to be very difficult for us to remember IP addresses but that just goes to show you the value of something that remembers for us which is DNS of course. But one of the other things that we need to remember that they've also built into IPv6 is something called IPsec which very appropriately stands for IP security and that is something that's going to be built in natively into the IPv6 protocol and IPsec as you'll see later provides really two distinct capabilities And that is authentication and encryption built right into natively into the IP version six protocol. Now, if you look at the next slide here is just a comparison of IP version 4 compared to IP version 6 the year that they were both developed the address size which goes from 32 bits to 128 bits now that number that 2 to the 128 represents is pretty large and so the experts are saying things like every grain of sand on earth can have its own IP address and we would still not run out and so that's going to be a tremendous number of IP addresses that we will be able to use when everybody's using IP version 6 but the significant thing from our perspective and yours is to understand that IP version 6 has security built-in through mandating the use of IPSec which again provides two distinct capabilities from a security perspective and you'll need to remember these authentication and encryption built right into the protocol itself now We are still at the network layer and so significant protocols that operate here at the network layer are of course IP which we just talked about which is like the captain of the team. But there's also some other ones like ICMP. ICMP is used for the messaging aspects of networking. So reporting troubles with routes. destination not reachable messages are handled by the ICMP protocol but as you can see on the slide here the ICMP protocol is also the basis for two very important commands that we have to focus on from a security perspective and they are ping and Traceroute. They both use the ICMP protocol to do what they do. Ping tries to see if a host is reachable, whether it's alive and operating. And that is much more information than you might be willing to allow people out on the internet. to be able to find out. In fact, Traceroute is much more dangerous because it tries to map exactly what your IP routing schemes look like. And so they try and map what your addressing schemes and network segments look like on the inside of the network. That's what the trace route command actually does. And that's why it's pretty standard for all organizations out there to block incoming ICMP traffic right at the firewall because you don't want potential attackers out there on the internet and the web to be able to know certain hosts that you may have that are operating and have them map what your network elements actually look like. So blocking incoming ICMP is pretty standard for all organizations. There are some other significant protocols that operate here like IPSec that we just talked about which is what is built in natively into the IPv6 protocol. We'll talk more about that later on as it is really a tunneling protocol that provides authentication and encryption. capabilities. IGMP is used for group membership information. So when routers need to learn about the segments that they control across networks, typically it is the IGMP Internet Group Membership Protocol that is used to provide that information and what you see here OSPF is just only one example of what we call routing protocols. Routing protocols are used by routers to make decisions. Think of them as the language that routers understand to be able to do what they do and that is route properly and in the best way possible. There are different languages that have been developed, in other words different routing protocols like BGP and RIP and OSPF and so on. They're just different languages. Some of them have more security than others. For example, it is pretty standard not to use RIP because it is considered to be very vulnerable. But those are routing protocols that also operate at the network layer here. So these are the significant devices and technologies that operate at layer two. Of course, this is the first layer within the OSI model where we can start making some significant security decisions. But very simple decisions and that is simply a router that makes decisions based on the header portion of that datagram That is what we call simple packet filtering firewalls, simply making decisions on what's available in the header portion such as source IP address, destination IP address and some other very simple types of decisions. Routers operate at the network layer of course as you know and specifically layer three switches which are basically switches that have been given some ability to do things at layer three but again caution on the exam unless they specifically mention layer 3 switches then you wouldn't assume that a switch operates at layer 3 in fact it does not it operates natively at layer 2 but at layer 3 switch is a switch that has some routing capabilities at layer 3
